explanation_md: `hi` is a `Holder<int>` storing 10; `hd` is a `Holder<double>` storing 3.5. Each `get()` returns its stored value. The two instantiations are independent classes generated from the same template, so the output is `10` then `3.5`.

prompt_md: Why must template member function definitions be placed in the header file (or the same translation unit where they are used), rather than in a separate `.cpp` file?

options.a: Header files are compiled first, so the linker can find them.
options.b: The compiler needs the full template definition to generate code for each type it is instantiated with; a definition hidden in a `.cpp` file is invisible to other translation units.
options.c: Template functions are slower in `.cpp` files because the optimizer cannot inline them.
options.d: The `template` keyword is only legal inside `.h` files.

explanation_md: When the compiler sees `Holder<int>` in a translation unit, it must immediately generate the `Holder<int>` class — constructors, methods, everything. If the definitions are in a separate `.cpp` file, that translation unit never sees them, so no code is generated and the linker reports undefined symbols.

options.a: The constructor is missing its body — it should say `= default`.
options.b: Out-of-class definitions use `Wrap::` but should use `Wrap<T>::` to name the template instantiation.
options.c: `template <typename T>` is required only on the class definition, not on out-of-class member definitions.
options.d: `T` cannot be used as a return type outside the class body.

explanation_md: When defining a template member function outside the class, the class name must carry the template parameter: `Wrap<T>::Wrap(T data)` and `T Wrap<T>::value() const`. Writing `Wrap::Wrap` or `Wrap::value` is a compile error because `Wrap` alone is not a complete type — `Wrap<T>` is.
`Swapper<int>` is initialized with `m_a = 1` and `m_b = 2`. After `swap()`, `m_a = 2` and `m_b = 1`. `first()` returns 2 and `second()` returns 1, so the output is `2 1`.

prompt_md: Which of the following correctly instantiates `template <typename T> class Box`?

options.a: `Box b{ 42 };` — the compiler deduces T = int from the constructor argument.
options.b: `Box<int> b{ 42 };` — T is explicitly specified as int.
options.c: `Box<> b{ 42 };` — empty angle brackets tell the compiler to deduce T.
options.d: `Box<auto> b{ 42 };` — auto deduces T from the initializer.

explanation_md: Class templates require explicit type arguments in angle brackets unless Class Template Argument Deduction (CTAD) applies. Option b is the straightforward, always-correct form. Option a may or may not work depending on whether the compiler can deduce T — it is not guaranteed. Options c and d are not valid syntax for template instantiation.
